title: The 10 Best Herbal Teas for Instant Stomach Cramps Relief
intro_desc: Herbal teas for stomach cramps are natural remedies made from various plant extracts that help alleviate abdominal pain and discomfort. They work by reducing inflammation, relaxing muscles, and soothing the digestive system. The best herbal teas for stomach cramps include Zingiber officinale, Foeniculum vulgare, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Cinchona officinalis, Mentha x piperita, Matricaria chamomilla, Artemisia absinthium, Peumus boldus, Ginkgo biloba, and Urtica dioica. If left untreated, stomach cramps can lead to missed workdays, social isolation, and a reduced quality of life, causing significant distress and discomfort for those affected.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale tea, also known as ginger tea, is a popular remedy for stomach cramps. Ginger contains a chemical called gingerol, which helps to reduce inflammation in the stomach and intestines. When you have stomach cramps, the muscles in your stomach and intestines contract and relax too quickly, causing pain. Ginger helps to slow down this process, giving your stomach and intestines time to relax and reducing the pain. Ginger tea also has a soothing effect on the stomach and intestines, helping to calm nausea and vomiting, which often accompany stomach cramps. Additionally, ginger has natural antispasmodic properties, which help to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, preventing them from contracting too quickly and causing more pain. By drinking ginger tea, you can help to ease stomach cramps and get relief from the discomfort.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerols are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce pain and discomfort.

Shogaols are good for stomach cramps because they help to relax muscles and reduce inflammation in the stomach, making them an effective natural remedy for relieving pain and discomfort caused by cramps.
Zingerone is good for stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and inflammation in the stomach, thereby providing relief from cramps.
Polyphenols, also known as Yarrow, are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and swelling in the digestive tract.

remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea, also known as yarrow tea, and help with stomach cramps, start by gathering fresh or dried yarrow leaves and flowers. If using fresh yarrow, cut it into small pieces and dry it in a low-temperature oven or by air drying it for a few days. To make the tea, take one teaspoon of dried yarrow and add it to a cup of boiling water. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, then strain the tea into another cup. You can also add honey or lemon to taste, but be sure to drink it while it's still warm. The recommended dose is 1-2 cups per day, and it's best to consult with a healthcare professional before using yarrow tea if you have any underlying health conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita tea, also known as peppermint tea, is a natural remedy for stomach cramps. The tea has a compound called menthol, which helps relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines. This relaxation reduces the spasms and cramps that can cause pain and discomfort. Additionally, menthol has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce swelling and irritation in the digestive tract. When consumed as a tea, peppermint oil is released, which can help ease nausea and indigestion. As a result, peppermint tea can provide quick relief from stomach cramps caused by digestive issues, such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) or food poisoning. Drinking peppermint tea may also help calm the digestive system, reducing the frequency and severity of stomach cramps.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Rosmarinic acid is good for stomach cramps because it helps to reduce inflammation and relax the muscles in the stomach, which in turn eases the cramping and discomfort.
Menthol is good for stomach cramps because it helps to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing pain and discomfort.
Menthone is good for stomach cramps because it helps relax the muscles in the digestive system, reducing spasms and discomfort.

remedies.image_alt: Mentha x piperita herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea that helps with stomach cramps, start by gathering dried yarrow flowers, also known as Achillea millefolium. You can buy these from a health food store or grow them in your garden. Begin by boiling a cup of water, then add one to two teaspoons of dried yarrow flowers to the water. Reduce the heat to a simmer and let it steep for five to ten minutes. Strain the mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup, discarding the solids. You can also add a bit of honey to sweeten the taste if needed. Once the tea has cooled slightly, drink it slowly to help soothe stomach cramps and relax the digestive system. It is recommended to drink this tea in moderation, ideally two to three times a day, to achieve the desired effects.

remedies.remedy_desc: Glycyrrhiza glabra tea, also known as licorice root tea, is a natural remedy that can help with stomach cramps. This tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can reduce swelling and ease pain in the digestive tract. Glycyrrhizin, a compound found in licorice root, has been shown to protect the stomach lining from acid damage and reduce inflammation. This can help to alleviate cramps and discomfort caused by stomach ulcers or acid reflux. Additionally, licorice root tea has a soothing effect on the digestive system, helping to calm stomach spasms and relax the muscles in the abdominal area. This can help to provide quick relief from stomach cramps and other digestive issues. The antispasmodic properties of licorice root tea can also help to regulate bowel movements and prevent cramping associated with irritable bowel syndrome (IBS).

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Glycyrrhizin is good for stomach cramps because it helps to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce inflammation, which can help to ease the pain and discomfort caused by cramps.
Saponins are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help soothe and relax the stomach muscles, reducing pain and discomfort.

Triterpenoids are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory and relaxant properties that help to reduce pain and spasms in the digestive system, making it easier to manage and alleviate stomach discomfort.
Polyphenols are good for stomach cramps because they help relax muscles and reduce inflammation in the digestive system, making it easier to manage cramps and discomfort.
Tannins are good for stomach cramps because they help to soothe and calm the stomach muscles, reducing inflammation and pain by acting as a natural anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic agent.

remedies.image_alt: Glycyrrhiza glabra herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea, also known as yarrow tea, which helps with stomach cramps, you will need some dried yarrow flowers. Start by boiling fresh water in a pot. Then, add one to two teaspoons of dried yarrow flowers to the boiling water. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flowers to infuse their medicinal properties into the water. After the steeping time, carefully strain the tea into a cup to remove the flowers. You can use a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth for this purpose. Once strained, your yarrow tea is ready to drink. It's recommended to drink it in small amounts, about 1/2 cup, 2-3 times a day, especially when you're experiencing stomach cramps. You can also add honey or lemon to taste, but make sure to consult with a healthcare professional before consuming yarrow tea, especially if you're pregnant, breastfeeding, or have any underlying medical conditions.

remedies.remedy_desc: Foeniculum vulgare tea, also known as fennel tea, is a natural remedy for stomach cramps. The tea is made from the seeds of the fennel plant, which contain a compound called anethole. Anethole helps to relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, which can become spasmodic and crampy due to various reasons like gas, indigestion, or menstruation. By calming these muscles, fennel tea reduces the discomfort and pain associated with stomach cramps. The tea also has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to soothe the stomach lining and reduce inflammation. Additionally, fennel tea is a natural carminative, meaning it helps to release trapped gas in the digestive system, which can contribute to stomach cramps. By promoting digestion and reducing inflammation, fennel tea can provide relief from stomach cramps and other digestive issues.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Polyphenols are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce pain and discomfort.

Anethole is good for stomach cramps because it has a natural ability to relax the muscles in the digestive tract and reduce inflammation, which helps to ease the discomfort and pain associated with cramps.

Tannins are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce pain and swelling in the digestive tract, and also have a calming effect on the muscles that cause cramps.
Foeniculin is good for stomach cramps because it helps relax the muscles in the stomach and reduces inflammation, which in turn eases the cramping and discomfort.

remedies.image_alt: Foeniculum vulgare herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal teas that help with stomach cramps, start by gathering dried or fresh leaves and flowers of the plant, also known as Yarrow. If using fresh, dry the leaves and flowers by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. Once dry, measure out one to two teaspoons of the dried plant material for every eight ounces of boiling water. Pour the boiling water over the plant material in a teapot or a mug, and let it steep for five to ten minutes. Strain the tea into another container, discarding the solids, and add honey or lemon to taste if needed. Drink the tea hot or warm, ideally three times a day, to help soothe stomach cramps and reduce inflammation. It's also essential to note that you can combine Achillea millefolium with other herbal teas, such as peppermint or chamomile, to create a more soothing and relaxing drink.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cinchona officinalis tea is a good remedy for stomach cramps because it has natural ingredients that can help relax the muscles in your stomach. The tea contains quinine, a compound that has anti-inflammatory properties. This means it can reduce swelling and ease pain in your stomach. When you drink Cinchona officinalis tea, the quinine and other ingredients can help calm your stomach muscles and stop cramps. It can also help lower your body temperature, which can be helpful if you have cramps caused by a fever or infection. Additionally, Cinchona officinalis tea has a soothing effect on your digestive system, which can help ease cramps and discomfort. Some people find that drinking Cinchona officinalis tea helps to reduce the severity and frequency of stomach cramps, making it a popular natural remedy for this common problem.

First, boil a cup of water and then add one teaspoon of dried yarrow flowers to the pot. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the flowers to release their medicinal properties into the water. Next, strain the tea using a strainer into a cup and discard the flowers. You can add honey to the tea if you prefer it sweet, but be sure to use it in moderation. To help relieve stomach cramps, drink the tea as soon as it cools down to a comfortable temperature. You can also store the dried yarrow flowers in an airtight container and make the tea as needed, but be sure to check the expiration date and the quality of the flowers before using them.

remedies.remedy_desc: Artemisia absinthium tea, also known as wormwood tea, has been used for centuries to ease stomach cramps. This is because the tea contains a compound called absinthin, which helps to relax the muscles in the digestive system. When we have stomach cramps, the muscles in our stomach become tight and spasm, causing pain. The absinthin in wormwood tea helps to calm down these spasms and relax the muscles, making it easier to pass gas and move food through the digestive system. As a result, the pain caused by stomach cramps is reduced, and the digestive system starts to function normally again. The anti-inflammatory properties of wormwood tea also help to reduce swelling and inflammation in the digestive tract, which can contribute to stomach cramps. Drinking wormwood tea can help to soothe and calm the digestive system, providing quick relief from stomach cramps.

Polyphenols are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce pain and discomfort.

If you don't have dried ones, you can dry fresh leaves and flowers by tying them in small bunches and hanging them upside down in a warm, dry place. Next, add one teaspoon of the dried herb to a cup of boiling water. Then, reduce the heat to a simmer and let the tea steep for 5-7 minutes. Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove the herb. Let the tea cool down a bit before drinking it. You can add honey or lemon to taste, if you like. It's recommended to drink 1-2 cups of the tea per day to help alleviate stomach cramps and other digestive issues.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis tea is a popular natural remedy for stomach cramps. This tea is made from the root of the valerian plant, which has been used for centuries to treat various health issues. The root contains compounds called valerenic acid and isovaleric acid, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties. These compounds help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce inflammation, providing relief from cramps and spasms. When you drink Valeriana officinalis tea, the compounds are absorbed into your body and work to calm the stomach muscles, reducing the pain and discomfort associated with stomach cramps. Valeriana officinalis tea also has a soothing effect on the nervous system, which can help to calm anxiety and stress that may be contributing to stomach cramps. Drinking a cup of Valeriana officinalis tea may help to ease stomach cramps and promote a sense of calm and well-being.

Achillea millefolium is a natural anti-inflammatory, so drinking this tea may help soothe and calm your stomach cramps. Some people like to drink it hot, but you can also let it cool down and drink it cold. Just remember to consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedy, especially if you have any underlying health conditions or take medications regularly.

remedies.remedy_desc: Matricaria chamomilla tea, also known as chamomile tea, is a natural remedy for stomach cramps. The active compounds in chamomile, such as apigenin and luteolin, have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce inflammation and spasms in the digestive tract. This can provide relief from stomach cramps, nausea, and diarrhea. Chamomile tea also has a soothing effect on the stomach lining, which can help to calm irritations and reduce discomfort. Additionally, chamomile's natural sedative properties can help to relax the muscles in the stomach, reducing spasms and cramps. Drinking chamomile tea can also help to regulate bowel movements and improve digestion, further reducing stomach cramps.

Luteolin helps with stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that reduce swelling and ease muscle spasms in the digestive tract.
Quercetin is good for stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help to relax the stomach muscles and reduce pain and discomfort.

remedies.image_alt: Matricaria chamomilla herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea that helps with stomach cramps, you will need dried yarrow flowers, a teapot, a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth, and a cup. Start by boiling water in the teapot, then add one teaspoon of dried yarrow flowers to the tea infuser or cheesecloth. Pour the boiling water over the flowers and let it steep for 5-7 minutes, depending on your desired tea strength. After the steeping time, carefully lift the infuser or cheesecloth out of the pot and pour the tea into your cup. Strain the tea if necessary to remove any remaining flowers. You can drink the tea hot or let it cool down to room temperature. It's recommended to drink 1-2 cups of yarrow tea per day to help with stomach cramps, but always consult with a healthcare professional before using any herbal remedy.

remedies.remedy_desc: Peumus boldus tea, also known as boldo tea, is a traditional remedy for stomach cramps. The tea is made from the leaves of the Peumus boldus tree, which is native to South America. The leaves contain compounds that help relax the muscles in the stomach and intestines, reducing spasms and cramps. These compounds, including boldine and glycosides, work by soothing the stomach lining and reducing inflammation. By calming the stomach muscles, Peumus boldus tea can provide quick relief from stomach cramps, making it a popular remedy for people experiencing nausea, diarrhea, and other digestive issues. Regular consumption of boldo tea may also help prevent stomach cramps by improving digestion and reducing stomach acid production.

Polyphenols are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to relax the stomach muscles and reduce pain.
Flavonoids are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce pain and inflammation.

remedies.image_alt: Peumus boldus herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea, start by gathering fresh or dried flowers of the plant, also known as yarrow. If using fresh flowers, pick them in the morning when they are at their best, and gently rinse them with water to remove any dirt. If using dried flowers, simply open a packet or jar and measure out one teaspoon for every cup of boiling water you plan to use. Next, heat a cup of water in a teapot or kettle until it's boiling, then carefully pour it over the yarrow flowers in a cup or teapot. Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, which will allow the active ingredients to infuse into the water. After the steeping time has passed, carefully strain the tea into another cup to remove the flowers, and discard the solids. Your yarrow tea is now ready to drink, and can be enjoyed hot or cold, sweetened with honey if desired. To help with stomach cramps, drink 1-2 cups of the tea as needed, ideally when the cramps are at their worst.

remedies.remedy_desc: Achillea millefolium tea, also known as yarrow tea, is a natural remedy for stomach cramps. The tea is made from the flowers of the yarrow plant, which contain compounds like flavonoids and terpenoids. These compounds help to relax the muscles in the stomach and reduce inflammation, which can cause cramps. They also have a calming effect on the digestive system, which can help to ease nausea and spasms. When consumed as a tea, yarrow's active compounds are absorbed into the body and can help to block the production of prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that can cause stomach contractions and pain. This natural anti-inflammatory effect can provide quick relief from stomach cramps, and may also help to prevent future episodes.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Tannins are good for stomach cramps because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce muscle spasms and soothe the digestive tract, thereby easing cramps and discomfort.
Quercetin helps to ease stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that reduce swelling in the stomach muscles and relax them, allowing for relief from pain and discomfort.
Apigenin is good for stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and spasms in the digestive system.

remedies.image_alt: Achillea millefolium herbal teas for stomach cramps
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Achillea millefolium herbal tea for stomach cramps, start by gathering a handful of dried or fresh yarrow flowers. If using fresh flowers, rinse them gently with cold water and pat dry with a paper towel. Combine 1 tablespoon of the dried flowers or 2 tablespoons of the fresh flowers with 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot or a heat-resistant cup. Let it steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the herbal goodness to infuse. Strain the tea into another cup using a tea strainer or a piece of cheesecloth, and discard the solids. You can add honey to taste, if desired, to make the tea more palatable. Once ready, drink the tea warm or at room temperature, ideally between 2-3 times a day, to help alleviate stomach cramps and soothe digestive issues.

supplementary_best: For stomach cramps, a combination of 'Foeniculum vulgare' (fennel tea) and 'Mentha x piperita' (peppermint tea) is highly recommended. Fennel tea helps to relax the muscles in the stomach, reducing spasms and cramps. Peppermint tea, on the other hand, has anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the digestive tract and ease cramp-related discomfort. Together, they work to calm the stomach and provide fast relief from cramps. Additionally, adding a small amount of 'Glycyrrhiza glabra' (licorice root tea) to this combination can enhance its effectiveness, as licorice root has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce swelling and discomfort in the stomach. However, it's essential to note that licorice root should be consumed in moderation due to its potential side effects.
supplementary_avoid: If you have stomach cramps, there are some herbal teas you should avoid. One of these is peppermint tea made from 'Agastache mexicana' (Mexican hyssop) as it can irritate the stomach and make cramps worse. Another is 'Cassia auriculata' (Tulsi or Holy Basil) tea, which can cause stomach upset and worsen cramps. You should also avoid 'Silybum marianum' (Milk Thistle) tea as it can cause stomach irritation and increase cramps. 'Aristolochia clematitidis' (Dutchman's Pipe) tea is also not recommended as it can cause stomach ulcers and worsen cramps. Finally, 'Rauvolfia serpentina' (Indian Snakeroot) tea should be avoided as it can cause stomach upset and worsen cramps.
supplementary_other_remedies: Aloe vera gel can help soothe stomach cramps because it has anti-inflammatory properties that calm the digestive system. When applied topically to the stomach area, it can also provide relief from cramping and discomfort. Another natural remedy is ginger. Ginger has been used for centuries to treat nausea and stomach pain. It works by slowing down digestion, which helps ease cramps and spasms in the stomach. Ginger can also help reduce inflammation and kill bacteria that may be causing the stomach pain. Additionally, probiotics found in fermented foods like yogurt, kefir, and sauerkraut can help alleviate stomach cramps by restoring the balance of good bacteria in the gut. This balance is crucial for a healthy digestive system and can help reduce inflammation and pain. Some people also find relief by eating bananas, as they are high in potassium, which helps relax the muscles in the stomach.
supplementary_related_ailments: People who experience stomach cramps may also have irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). IBS can cause bloating, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. Medicinal herbs like peppermint and ginger can help. Peppermint relaxes the muscles in the digestive tract, reducing cramps and spasms. Ginger soothes inflammation and calms the digestive system.  Some people with stomach cramps may also experience acid reflux, a condition where stomach acid flows back up into the esophagus. This can cause heartburn and discomfort. Licorice root and slippery elm can help. Licorice root reduces inflammation in the digestive tract and soothes the stomach lining, while slippery elm protects and heals the esophagus.  Stomach cramps can also be a symptom of menstrual cramps in women. Cramping is caused by prostaglandins, hormone-like substances that cause the uterus to contract. Feverfew and cramp bark can help. Feverfew reduces prostaglandin production, while cramp bark relaxes the uterus and eases cramps.  Stomach cramps can also be a sign of an underlying infection, such as food poisoning or a bacterial infection like salmonella or E. coli. In these cases, herbs like yarrow and plantain may help. Yarrow reduces inflammation and promotes healing, while plantain soothes the digestive tract and reduces diarrhea.  Finally, stomach cramps can be a symptom of stress and anxiety, which can exacerbate digestive problems. Herbs like chamomile and lemon balm can help. Chamomile calms the nervous system and reduces anxiety, while lemon balm soothes the digestive system and promotes relaxation.
